steps.title: 96-well flat bottom plate (Anicrin, catalog number
steps.instruction: 96-well flat bottom plate (Anicrin, catalog number: M09600P

steps.title: Ice buckets for cooling steps
steps.instruction: Ice buckets for cooling steps

steps.title: Sacrifice two mice by cervical dislocation...
steps.instruction: Sacrifice two mice by cervical dislocation and immediately dissect the brains. Note: Treat each brain separately in different tubes for the following steps.

steps.title: Place the brain in a pre-chilled...
steps.instruction: Place the brain in a pre-chilled tissue grinding pestle and add 8 mL of ice-cold sucrose buffer.

steps.title: Homogenize at 900 rpm using 12...
steps.instruction: Homogenize at 900 rpm using 12 up-and-down strokes with the homogenizer.

steps.title: Transfer the homogenate into a 15 mL centrifuge tube
steps.instruction: Transfer the homogenate into a 15 mL centrifuge tube.

steps.title: Spin the homogenate at 900× g...
steps.instruction: Spin the homogenate at 900× g for 10 min at 4 °C to remove large debris.

steps.title: Transfer the supernatant (S
steps.instruction: Transfer the supernatant (S

steps.title: into a 15 mL centrifuge tube...
steps.instruction: into a 15 mL centrifuge tube and discard the pellet (P1).

steps.title: Spin the collected supernatant (S
steps.instruction: Spin the collected supernatant (S

steps.title: in a centrifuge at 10,000× g...
steps.instruction: in a centrifuge at 10,000× g for 15 min at 4 °C.

steps.title: Discard the supernatant (S
steps.instruction: Discard the supernatant (S

steps.title: and resuspend the pellet (P
steps.instruction: and resuspend the pellet (P

steps.title: in 2 mL of ice-cold sucrose...
steps.instruction: in 2 mL of ice-cold sucrose buffer by gently pipetting up and down 3–4 times.

steps.title: Add 6 mL of ice-cold sucrose...
steps.instruction: Add 6 mL of ice-cold sucrose buffer and gently invert to mix.

steps.title: Spin the sample at 15,000× g...
steps.instruction: Spin the sample at 15,000× g for 15 min at 4 °C.

steps.title: Discard the supernatant (S2’) and resuspend the pellet (P2’
steps.instruction: Discard the supernatant (S2’) and resuspend the pellet (P2’: crude synaptosomes) in 2 mL of sucrose buffer (see graphical overview for a schematic representation of synaptosomes preparation).

steps.title: Combine the crude synaptosomes obtained from...
steps.instruction: Combine the crude synaptosomes obtained from two brains, 4 mL in total, in a single 15 mL tube. B. Protein estimation (Bradford assay)

steps.title: Mix the Bradford reagent well before...
steps.instruction: Mix the Bradford reagent well before use and allow it to come to room temperature.

steps.title: Prepare protein standards using a stock...
steps.instruction: Prepare protein standards using a stock solution of 2 mg/mL BSA in sucrose buffer as mentioned in Table 1 . Table

steps.title: Dilution scheme for protein standards using...
steps.instruction: Dilution scheme for protein standards using 2 mg/mL of BSA in sucrose buffer. Vial Sucrose buffer Volume and source of BSA Final BSA concentration A 0 300 μL of stock 2 mg/mL B 125 μL 370 μL of stock 1.5 mg/mL C 325 μL 325 μL of stock 1 mg/mL D 175 μL 175 μL of vial B dilution 0.75 mg/mL E 325 μL 325 μL of vial C dilution 0.5 mg/mL F 325 μL 325 μL of vial E dilution 0.25 mg/mL G 400 μL 0 0 mg/mL (blank)

steps.title: Add 200 μL of Bradford reagent...
steps.instruction: Add 200 μL of Bradford reagent to a 96-well plate, add 2 μL of your protein standards or your sample (crude synaptosomes) to the reagent, and mix. Note: Perform each measurement in triplicate and ensure that your actual sample concentration falls within the linear range of your standard curve, as other concentrations may lead to a nonlinear response.

steps.title: Incubate for 5 min at room temperature in the dark
steps.instruction: Incubate for 5 min at room temperature in the dark.

steps.title: Measure absorbance at 595 nm using the SpectroStar Nano V5
steps.instruction: Measure absorbance at 595 nm using the SpectroStar Nano V5.

steps.title: Determine protein concentration using the MARS data analysis software
steps.instruction: Determine protein concentration using the MARS data analysis software. Note: The usual protein concentration obtained from a single P60 mouse brain is 2–3 mg/mL (if the synaptosome yield is lower than expected, please refer to the troubleshooting section). C. Proteolytic digestion procedure

steps.title: For the untreated sample, add 5...
steps.instruction: For the untreated sample, add 5 mg of crude synaptosomes in a final volume of 10 mL of sucrose buffer without adding trypsin.

steps.title: To initiate proteolytic digestion and to...
steps.instruction: To initiate proteolytic digestion and to reach a final protein–protease ratio of 100:1 (see troubleshooting section), add 500 µL of trypsin solution (0.1 mg/mL) to 5 mg of crude synaptosomes in a final volume of 10 mL of sucrose buffer (see troubleshooting section).

steps.title: Incubate the untreated sample and the...
steps.instruction: Incubate the untreated sample and the protein–protease mixture for 10 min at 30 °C in a water bath, with occasional gentle inversion (no vortexing).

steps.title: Centrifuge the samples at 8,700× g...
steps.instruction: Centrifuge the samples at 8,700× g for 3 min at 4 °C.

steps.title: Quickly resuspend the pellets in 1...
steps.instruction: Quickly resuspend the pellets in 1 mL of sucrose buffer containing 200 μL of 6× loading sample buffer to stop protease activity and in order to reach a final concentration of 5 µg/µL.

steps.title: Boil the samples at 95 °C for 10 min
steps.instruction: Boil the samples at 95 °C for 10 min.

steps.title: The samples are now ready for...
steps.instruction: The samples are now ready for SDS-PAGE to determine the subcellular localization of your protein of interest. Pause point: Samples can be stored at -20 °C, and the following steps can be performed the day after. In our hands, samples can be stored at -20 °C for three months without degradation. D. SDS gel casting and running

steps.title: Clean the glass plates thoroughly and...
steps.instruction: Clean the glass plates thoroughly and insert them into the casting frame.

steps.title: Prepare the 10% separating gel solution...
steps.instruction: Prepare the 10% separating gel solution ( Table 2 ) in a 15 mL tube and swirl the solution gently. Table

steps.title: Recipe for 10% separating gel Reagent...
steps.instruction: Recipe for 10% separating gel Reagent Quantity or Volume Water 3 mL 4× separating buffer 1.875 mL Acrylamide/bis-acrylamide 2.5 mL 10% APS solution 150 μL TEMED 15 μL

steps.title: Pipette the separating gel solution between...
steps.instruction: Pipette the separating gel solution between the glass plates, leaving 2 cm at the top.

steps.title: Overlay the separating gel with isopropanol,...
steps.instruction: Overlay the separating gel with isopropanol, making the separating gel regular and linear.

steps.title: Once set, pour off completely the...
steps.instruction: Once set, pour off completely the isopropanol and let it dry for 5 min.

steps.title: Prepare the stacking gel solution (...
steps.instruction: Prepare the stacking gel solution ( Table 3 ) in a 15 mL tube and swirl the solution gently. Table

steps.title: Recipe for 3
steps.instruction: Recipe for 3.8% stacking gel Reagent Quantity or Volume Water 1.625 mL 4× stacking buffer 0.625 mL Acrylamide/bis-acrylamide 0.333 mL 10% APS solution 75 μL TEMED 7.5 μL

steps.title: Pipette the stacking gel solution into...
steps.instruction: Pipette the stacking gel solution into the gap between the glass plates, layering it on top of the set separating gel.

steps.title: Quickly insert the comb, ensuring to...
steps.instruction: Quickly insert the comb, ensuring to avoid any air bubbles, and allow it to sit for 10 min to set.

steps.title: Carefully remove the comb once the...
steps.instruction: Carefully remove the comb once the stacking gel is fully set.

steps.title: Take the gel out of the...
steps.instruction: Take the gel out of the casting stand and insert it into the running cell buffer dam.

steps.title: Pour 1× running buffer into the...
steps.instruction: Pour 1× running buffer into the inner and outer chambers of the gel running apparatus and remove the comb.

steps.title: Load the untreated and trypsin-treated samples...
steps.instruction: Load the untreated and trypsin-treated samples (20–30 μg) into the wells along with a protein ladder/marker for size reference.

steps.title: Run the gel at 80 V...
steps.instruction: Run the gel at 80 V for the staking phase (approximately 40 min) and then increase to 130 V for the resolving phase, running until the dye front reaches the bottom of the gel. E. Western blotting

steps.title: Prepare the transfer materials by soaking...
steps.instruction: Prepare the transfer materials by soaking the sponge, blotting paper, and nitrocellulose (NC) membrane in cold 1× transfer buffer.

steps.title: Assemble the sandwich for the transfer...
steps.instruction: Assemble the sandwich for the transfer in the following order: sponge—blotting paper—NC membrane—blotting paper—sponge.

steps.title: Use a roller to gently remove...
steps.instruction: Use a roller to gently remove any air bubbles between the layers for even transfer.

steps.title: Fill the transfer chamber with cold...
steps.instruction: Fill the transfer chamber with cold 1× transfer buffer and insert the cool pack to maintain the temperature.

steps.title: Insert the sandwich into the transfer...
steps.instruction: Insert the sandwich into the transfer chamber and run the transfer at 110 V for 100 min in a cold room or with the chamber on ice to prevent overheating. F. Membrane staining

steps.title: Carefully remove the NC membrane after...
steps.instruction: Carefully remove the NC membrane after the transfer and immediately stain it with Ponceau S staining solution for a few minutes to visualize the protein bands.

steps.title: Rinse the membrane with water and...
steps.instruction: Rinse the membrane with water and take an image to record the transferred protein bands (see Figure 1 ).

steps.title: Wash the membrane several times with...
steps.instruction: Wash the membrane several times with 1× PBST until all traces of Ponceau S stain are gone.

steps.title: Block the membrane by incubating it...
steps.instruction: Block the membrane by incubating it in blocking solution for 30–60 min to prevent nonspecific antibody binding.

steps.title: While the membrane is blocking, prepare...
steps.instruction: While the membrane is blocking, prepare the primary antibody solution in a 15 mL tube using the blocking solution at the required dilution (see Table 4 ). Table

steps.title: Antibodies dilutions used in Figure 1...
steps.instruction: Antibodies dilutions used in Figure 1 Antibody Dilution used Source GluR1 1:1,000 Mouse GluR2 1:1,000 Mouse GluN2A 1:200 Rabbit GluN2B 1:200 Rabbit Synaptotagmin1 1:500 Rabbit Synaptophysin1 1:500 Mouse SNAP-25 1:500 Mouse

steps.title: Transfer the membrane into the tube...
steps.instruction: Transfer the membrane into the tube containing the primary antibody solution and incubate overnight at 4 °C on a rotator to ensure uniform binding.

steps.title: The following day, wash the membrane...
steps.instruction: The following day, wash the membrane with 1× PBST three times for 10 min each to remove any unbound primary antibodies.

steps.title: Prepare the secondary antibody (mouse or...
steps.instruction: Prepare the secondary antibody (mouse or rabbit, depending on the primary antibody used) solution at the dilution of 1:10,000 in 1× PBST.

steps.title: Incubate the membrane with the secondary...
steps.instruction: Incubate the membrane with the secondary antibody for 45 min to 1 h at room temperature.

steps.title: Wash the membrane with 1× PBST...
steps.instruction: Wash the membrane with 1× PBST three times for 10 min each and then twice with 1× PBS to remove unbound secondary antibodies.

steps.title: Visualize the membrane using the LiCOR...
steps.instruction: Visualize the membrane using the LiCOR imaging system to detect the protein of interest (see Figure 1 ) (refer to the troubleshooting section if variability in the proteolytic cleavage of postsynaptic proteins, unexpected degradation, or digestion of presynaptic proteins is observed). Figure

steps.title: Tryptic digest of synaptosomes reveals subcellular localization of synaptic proteins
steps.instruction: Tryptic digest of synaptosomes reveals subcellular localization of synaptic proteins. A. Schematic representation of synaptosomes showing the presynaptic terminal resealed into an enclosed compartment, protecting presynaptic proteins from trypsin proteolysis, while postsynaptic proteins remain susceptible to tryptic digestion. B. Synaptosomes are either left untreated or incubated with trypsin, followed by immunoblot analysis. Presynaptic proteins, such as synaptotagmin1 (Syt1), SNAP-25, and synaptophysin1 (Syp), are protected from proteolysis. In contrast, postsynaptic proteins, including the AMPAR subunits GluA1 and GluA2, as well as the NMDAR subunits GluN2A and GluN2B, are sensitive to tryptic digestion, indicating their postsynaptic localization. C. Example of a Ponceau S staining of synaptosomes, either untreated or incubated with trypsin for 10 min at 30 °C. Please note that in the trypsin-treated sample, only postsynaptic proteins are cleaved. Therefore, there should not be a significant difference from the untreated sample. If many bands disappear, this may indicate over-digestion (see troubleshooting section).

description: Validation of protocol To our knowledge, this protocol or parts of it has been used and validated in the following research articles: Boyken et al. [1]. Molecular Profiling of Synaptic Vesicle Docking Sites Reveals Novel Proteins but Few Differences between Glutamatergic and GABAergic Synapses. Neuron ( Figure 1 , panel B; Figure 2 , panel A). The protocol was originally developed for the isolation of a fraction highly enriched in synaptic vesicles docked to active zone. Mild proteolysis of synaptosomes was used to dissociate the presynaptic from the postsynaptic membrane. Awasthi et al. [17]. Synaptotagmin-3 drives AMPA receptor endocytosis, depression of synapse strength, and forgetting. Science ( Figure 1 , panel E and F). The protocol was used to verify the subcellular localization of synaptotagmin-3 (Syt-3). Awasthi et al. demonstrated that presynaptic proteins (such as synapsin, synaptobrevin-2, and Rab3a) were protected from trypsin cleavage, while postsynaptic proteins (such as Homer, GluA1, and PSD95), including Syt3, were cleaved. Azarnia Tehran et al. [18]. Selective endocytosis of Ca 2+ -permeable AMPARs by the Alzheimer’s disease risk factor CALM bidirectionally controls synaptic plasticity. Science Advances ( Figure 1 , panel B and C). The protocol was used to reveal the subcellular localization of the endocytic adaptor clathrin assembly lymphoid myeloid leukemia protein (CALM). We consistently found that while presynaptic proteins (such as SNAP-25, synaptophysin, and Rab3a) were protected from proteolysis, CALM was sensitive to trypsin, indicative of a large postsynaptic pool. Li et al. [20]. P85S6K sustains synaptic GluA1 to ameliorate cognitive deficits in Alzheimer’s disease. Translational Neurodegeneration ( Figure 1 , panel C). The protocol was used to verify the subcellular localization of p85S6K. As expected, presynaptic proteins (such as synaptophysin) were protected, while postsynaptic proteins (such as GluA1 and PSD95) were cleaved. Li et al
